title: Carving patterns into leather-hard clay
subtitle: Incised and relief carving on clay at the leather-hard stage
excerpt: Carving at leather-hard is surface decoration that removes clay rather than pressing into it. Incised carving cuts grooves into the surface; relief carving cuts away the background to leave a raised design. Both work best at the leather-hard stage when the clay holds a clean cut without tearing or crumbling. No kiln or wheel is needed.
sourceNotes: Clay carving technique. Standard surface decoration method. Technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: A carving technique that cuts grooves into a clay surface without removing the surrounding area.
Carving away the background clay so the design stands raised above the surface.
The optimal drying stage for carving: firm enough for a clean cut, damp enough for the tool to move smoothly.

content.text: carving draws lines into the clay surface.
takes more time but produces a design that stands away from the surface. Both work at

stage, when the clay has stiffened enough to hold a clean line but is not yet so dry that the walls crumble. Air-dry clay and paper clay both carve at this stage; paper clay is slightly more forgiving at thin walls. No kiln or wheel is needed.

attrs.title: Tool choice
attrs.body: A needle tool draws fine incised lines. A sgraffito tool (or a linoleum V-cutter) removes a small V-channel of clay cleanly. A loop tool removes larger areas of clay for relief work and is the tool for cutting away background. All three tools can be found in basic pottery starter sets.

content.text: Draw the design onto the leather-hard surface with a light scratch from the needle tool. Then cut the lines to final depth with the sgraffito tool or needle tool, pressing firmly and moving in a single continuous stroke. For a V-profile groove (visible from the side), hold the tool at 45 degrees on each side of the line. For a U-profile (round-bottomed), use a round-tipped tool and press straight down.

Sketch the design with a light needle tool mark. Using the loop tool, remove the clay from the background, the area around the raised design, to a depth of 3 to 5 mm. Work from the edge of the design inward. Keep the background even: an uneven background looks chaotic rather than deliberate. The raised design reads as the unpainted original surface of the clay.

Clay removed by the loop or sgraffito tool accumulates at the edges of the cut as small rolls or crumbs. Brush these away with a dry soft brush as you work. Do not use a wet sponge inside the carved design, it blurs the edges. Once the piece has dried to bone-dry, any remaining surface dust can be brushed and lightly sanded.
Geometric incised lines, parallel, cross-hatched, or diagonal, are the easiest starting point and look intentional even when spacing is imperfect.
A simple raised botanical motif (a leaf or stem) suits relief carving on a flat tile.

items.fix: Mist the surface lightly and wait five minutes. The window for clean carving is leather-hard, not bone-dry. If the clay is already bone-dry, carving is possible but edges will crumble, use a sharp pointed tool and work carefully.

items.symptom: Loop tool tears the clay surface instead of cutting smoothly
items.cause: Clay too wet, or the loop tool used with a scraping motion rather than a pulling motion.
items.fix: Pull the loop tool toward you rather than pushing it away. Allow the clay to stiffen a further hour before retrying.
